System Architecture Overview ┌──────────────────────────────────────────── ──────────────────┐ │ CLIENT (Browser) │ │ React 19 + Vite + TypeScript │ │ State: Supabase (auth + profile) + localStorage (UI) │ └───────────────────────┬──────────────────── ──────────────────┘ [Page 2] │ HTTPS REST / WebSocket ┌───────────────────────▼──────────────────── ──────────────────┐ │ BACKEND (FastAPI / Python) │ │ main.py ←→ llm_service.py ←→ file_speaker.py │ │ crawler.py cache.py real_data.py │ └──────┬───────────────────────────────────── ──────────────────┘ │ ├── 1. OpenRouter (Gemma 4 31B / 26B) [Cloud, primary] ├── 2. Google AI Studio (Gemma 4 31B) [Cloud, secondary] └── 3. Ollama local (gemma4:e4b) [Local, fallback] Key Architectural Decisions Decision Rationale Multi -LLM failover Prevents outages when any single cloud API is rate - limited or down System/User role separation Eliminates prompt leakage — model instructions never appear in user output Supabase for auth + data Persistent multi -device sessions without a self - managed database ChromaDB for RAG Local, zero -latency vector search for document chat File-based roadmap cache Avoids redundant LLM calls — 7-day TTL per dream+year+branch Edge -TTS for podcast Free, high -quality Microsoft Neural voices; 8 Indian languages 2. Frontend Architecture [Page 3] Stack • React 19 with functional components and hooks • TypeScript for full type safety • Vite as the build tool (HMR, ES Modules) • Lucide React for iconography • Supabase JS Client for authentication and database Directory Structure frontend/ ├── index.html ├── index.tsx # React DOM entry point (HashRouter lives here) ├── App.tsx # Route definitions + global auth state ├── types.ts # All shared TypeScript interfaces │ ├── views/ # Page -level components (one per route) │ ├── Onboarding.tsx # Dream Discovery quiz + profile creation │ ├── Dashboard.tsx # Home hub with progress overview │ ├── RoadmapView.tsx # 4 -stage visual roadmap │ ├── Planner.tsx # Daily tasks + Pomodoro timer │ ├── RevisionEngine.tsx # Flashcard + spaced repetition │ ├── MentorChat.tsx # AI mentor chat with session history │ ├── FileSpeaker.tsx # Document upload, RAG chat, podcast │ ├── Opportunities.tsx # Opportunity radar cards │ ├── CareerPivot.tsx # Career transition analyser │ ├── Resources.tsx # YouTube / books / news feed │ └── DreamDiscovery.tsx # Standalone career quiz view │ [Page 4] ├── geminiService.ts # All HTTP calls to the FastAPI backend ├── resourceApiService.ts # YouTube Data API v3 + Google Books ├── flashcardService.ts # Flashcard CRUD + FSRS scheduling ├── taskRevisionService.ts # Task revision + FSRS ├── fsrsService.ts # FSRS v5 spaced -repetition algorithm ├── ebisuService.ts # Ebisu Bayesian memory model ├── rewardService.ts # XP points + streak calculation └── i18n.ts # Language map (8 Indian languages) State Management No global state library. State lives in: 1. Supabase — user profile, roadmap, task history (cloud -synced, persistent) 2. localStorage — UI preferences, Pomodoro state, recent chat sessions 3. React component state — ephemeral view state (loading flags, form inputs) Routing Uses HashRouter (#/path) for compatibility with static hosting (Vercel, GitHub Pages). Backend Architecture Stack • FastAPI (Python 3.11+) for async REST + WebSocket endpoints • httpx for all async HTTP calls (LLM APIs + web crawling) • ChromaDB for vector storage (RAG) • edge -tts for Text -to-Speech (podcast audio) • BeautifulSoup / Crawl4AI for web scraping Module Responsibilities File Responsibility main.py API route definitions, request validation, CORS llm_service.py All LLM calls, failover logic, prompt building file_speaker.py Document RAG, text extraction, podcast generation crawler.py Web scraping (roadmap.sh, Wikipedia, GFG) cache.py File-based JSON cache for roadmaps real_data.py Curated real -world career data (skills, salaries, tools) 4. LLM Routing Pipeline All AI calls flow through a 3-tier auto -failover system in llm_service.py: [Page 6] Request │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Tier 1: OpenRouter │ Gemma 4 31B (free tier) │ → Fallback: 26B model │ Timeout: 120s └────────────┬────────────────┘ │ FAIL ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Tier 2: Google AI Studio │ Gemma 4 31B via Gemini API │ systemInstruction support │ Timeout: 120s └────────────┬────────────────┘ │ FAIL ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Tier 3: Ollama (Local) │ gemma4:e4b (quantized) │ /api/chat → /api/generate │ Timeout: 180s └─────────────────────────────┘ _call_llm_chat() — Core Function python async def _call_llm_chat( messages: list[dict], # [{"role": "system"|"user"|"assistant", "content": "..."}] max_tokens: int = 1500, temperature: float = 0.7, attachment_b64: str = "", # Base64 image for multimodal [Page 7] attachment_type: str = "", # MIME type json_mode: bool = False # Enforces JSON output ) -> str Per-provider json_mode behaviour: Provider JSON Mechanism OpenRouter response_format: {"type": "json_object"} Gemini AI Studio responseMimeType: "application/json" in generationConfig Ollama /api/chat format: "json" key added only when True — never set to "" Ollama /api/generate format: "json" key added only when True — never set to "" For Gemini: System content goes to systemInstruction field, separated from contents. _call_llm( ) — Legacy Wrapper (Safe) python async def _call_llm(prompt: str, ...) -> str: messages = [ {"role": "system", "content": "You are a professional assistant. Return only the requested content."}, {"role": "user", "content": prompt} ] return await _call_llm_chat(messages, ...) Ensures no flat -string prompt ever reaches the LLM without role separation. 5. Feature Deep -Dives 5.1 Dream Discovery [Page 8] Purpose: Help new users identify their ideal career through a guided quiz. Flow: 1. User answers 5 -7 questions (interests, personality traits, environment) 2. GET /api/discover_dream?interests=...&personality=... 3. discover_dream_careers() → LLM returns 12 career objects: [{"dream": "...", "subjects": ["..."]}] 4. User selects one → stored in Supabase profile Fallback: 12 hardcoded career options if LLM fails completely. 5.2 Roadmap Generator Purpose: Generate a personalised 4 -stage career learning roadmap enriched with real web data. Flow: 1. GET /api/roadmap or WS /ws/roadmap (WebSocket streams progress messages) 2. crawler.py scrapes up to 3 career sites: roadmap.sh → Wikipedia → GeeksForGeeks 3. Crawled content (max 12,000 chars) injected into LLM context 4. LLM generates structured ROADMAP_SCHEMA JSON → validated + normalised → cached 7 days Roadmap Schema (per stage): • id, title, description, duration • subjects — 8-10 topics/tools • skills — 6 specific abilities • projects — 3 portfolio project ideas • resources — (populated by frontend from real_data.py) 5.3 Daily Planner & Task Engine [Page 9]

2. crawler.py scrapes up to 3 career sites: roadmap.sh → Wikipedia → GeeksForGeeks 3. Crawled content (max 12,000 chars) injected into LLM context 4. LLM generates structured ROADMAP_SCHEMA JSON → validated + normalised → cached 7 days Roadmap Schema (per stage): • id, title, description, duration • subjects — 8-10 topics/tools • skills — 6 specific abilities • projects — 3 portfolio project ideas • resources — (populated by frontend from real_data.py) 5.3 Daily Planner & Task Engine [Page 9] Purpose: Generate 5 context -aware daily tasks based on current roadmap stage. Flow: 1. POST /api/tasks → generate_tasks(dream, current_stage, subjects, count) 2. LLM returns: [{"title": "...", "type": "theory|hands -on|review"}] 3. main.py filters irrelevant tech tasks for non -tech careers, pads to requested count 4. Displayed in Planner.tsx with Pomodoro timer (25/5 min cycles) 5. XP awarded on completion via rewardService.ts 5.4 AI Mentor Chat Purpose: Persistent, multimodal AI career mentor responding naturally to any question. Flow: 1. POST /api/chat with full chat history + user profile + optional image/document 2. Personalised system prompt built: student name, dream, stage, language 3. Full conversation history passed as role -based messages → context -aware response 4. Multimodal: Images as inlineData (Gemini) or image_url (OpenRouter) Session Storage: Supabase (persistent, multi -device). Multiple sessions via sidebar. 5.5 Smart Quiz Engine Purpose: Generate 10 challenging, roadmap -aware MCQ questions. Flow: 1. POST /api/quiz with subject, tasks, stage description, stage concepts 2. LLM generates "Professional Junior" difficulty questions targeting exact stage concepts [Page 10] 3. Returns: [{question, options[4], correctAnswer (int 0 -3), explanation}] 4. Immediate feedback + explanations in RevisionEngine.tsx 5.6 File Speaker (RAG + Podcast) Purpose: Transform any document into interactive learning material. Document Ingestion: • Upload PDF/DOCX/TXT → text extracted → chunked (512 tokens, 64 overlap) • Chunks embedded via Gemini text-embedding -004 • Stored in ChromaDB keyed by source_id RAG Chat: • Question embedded → top -5 chunks by cosine similarity → passed to LLM • Answer grounded in document (not hallucinated) AI Transformations: summary, key_concepts, takeaways, questions, flashcards, m ethodology Podcast Generation: 1. Auto -detect document language (8 Indian languages) 2. LLM generates 2 -host dialogue script (Alex + Sam) 3. Each line synthesised via edge -tts (Microsoft Neural voices) 4. Segments concatenated → MP3 served at /api/filespeaker/audio/{file} 5. Live Interaction: Ask host a question mid -podcast → receive audio reply 5.7 Opportunity Radar Purpose: Surface 6 relevant internships, jobs, hackathons for the student's career. [Page 11] Flow: POST /api/opportunities → LLM generates 6 domain -specific opportunities → enriched with direct search URLs (LinkedIn, Internshala, Naukri, Unstop, SIH, Freelancer) 5.8 Career Pivot Analyser Purpose: Quantify the difficulty of switching careers with an actionable bridge plan. Output: transferPercentage (0- 100), transferableSkills, biggestGap, marketDemand, timeToTransition, bridgeP lan (3-step action plan) 5.9 Spaced Repetition (FSRS + Ebisu) FSRS v5 (flashcards): Stability + difficulty + retrievability model. Schedules next review after each answer. Ebisu (task revision): Bayesian Beta distribution decay model. Predicts recall probability; surfaces tasks due for review. 6. Data Flow Diagrams Roadmap Generation User (dream, year, branch) │ ▼ [Page 13] Cache Hit? ──YES──► Return cached │NO ▼ crawler.py: Scrape roadmap.sh / Wikipedia / GFG │ ▼ generate_roadmap() ├── System: ROADMAP_SCHEMA + instructions └── User: Student profile + crawled content (max 12k chars) │ ▼ _call_llm_chat() [3 -tier failover] │ ▼ _parse_roadmap_json() + _normalize_stage() │ ▼ cache.save_cache() → Return to RoadmapView.tsx RAG Document Chat Upload document → extract_text_from_file() → Chunk (512 tokens, 64 overlap) → Gemini embed → ChromaDB store (source_id) User question → Embed question → ChromaDB cosine similarity → top -5 chunks [Page 14] → LLM (System: RAG expert | User: question + context) → Grounded answer → FileSpeaker.tsx 8. Prompt Architecture & Leakage Prevention The Problem (Fixed) Flat-string prompts caused cloud LLMs to echo their own instructions (e.g., "Constraint 1: Return only JSON...") back as part of the output. The Fix: Strict System/User Role Separation python # Every LLM call now uses this structure: messages = [ {"role": "system", "content": "<instructions, constraints, persona>"}, {"role": "user", "content": "<user -specific data only>"} ] await _call_llm_chat(messages, ...) Gemini: System content → systemInstruction field (completely separated from contents). Rule: System = instructions only. User = data only. Never mix. 9. Caching Strategy Roadmap Cache (cache.py) • Storage: JSON files in backend/roadmap_cache/ • Key: MD5 hash of {dream}_{year}_{branch} • TTL: 7 days • Invalidation: DELETE /api/cache In-Memory Source Store (main.py) • _source_store: dict[source_id → {title, text, chunks}] [Page 15] • Populated on upload, used for podcast/transform to retrieve full text • Lifetime: Process lifetime (cleared on server restart) 10. VITE_YOUTUBE_API_KEY=AIza... VITE_BOOKS_API_KEY=AIza... 11. Known Issues & Fixes Issue Root Cause Fix Double " Ollama" log Two concurrent requests (roadmap + tasks); format: "" caused both to fail /api/chat Fixed: only add format: "json" key when json_mode=True ; omit entirely otherwise [Page 16] Issue Root Cause Fix Task request triggering roadmap Frontend calling /api/roadmap + /api/tasks concurrently; roadmap wins Ollama Ollama chat now works reliably for tasks LLM instruction echoing Flat-string prompts merged system + user content Strict System/User separation; systemInstr uction for Gemini Duplicate _build_pr ompt call generate_roadmap called _build _prompt twice Unified to single call Career summary shows prompt text Legacy _call_llm bypassed role separation Converted to _call_llm_chat with system/user roles Readme # KALAM SPARK - AI Career Intelligence Platform Powered by Google Gemma 4 > **A Submission for the Gemma 4 Hackathon** > > **Kalam Spark** is a comprehensive, AI -powered career mentoring platform designed to democratize career guidance for students. It offers personalized roadmaps, an interactive AI mentor chat, document intelligence, and scientifically -backed learning tools —all driven by th e power of **Gemma 4**. Gemma 4 Hackathon** > > **Kalam Spark** is a comprehensive, AI -powered career mentoring platform designed to democratize career guidance for students. It offers personalized roadmaps, an interactive AI mentor chat, document intelligence, and scientifically -backed learning tools —all driven by th e power of **Gemma 4**. [Page 17] [![License]( https://img.shields.io/badge/License -Apache_2.0 - blue.svg )](https://opensource.org/licenses/Apache -2.0) --- ## Overall Summary (Agentic AI) Kalam Spark is built on an **Agentic AI Architecture** . Instead of being a simple chatbot, it utilizes a swarm of specialized AI agents that perceive data, reason over student goals, and execute complex workflows: - **Career Architect Agent:** Acts as the core engine. It autonomously crawls industry sources (Crawl4AI), analyzes real -time trends, and constructs a progressive 4 -stage learning roadmap. - **Mentor & Coach Agent:** A persistent multimodal agent that maintains session memory. It reasons about the student's education level and current branch to provide context -aware technical guidance. - **Document Intelligence Agent (File Speaker):** Specialized in RAG (Retrieval -Augmented Generation). It ingests complex documents, extracts key concepts, and can even act as a podcast host by scriptwriting and narrating study material. - **Opportunity Radar Agent:** Proactively maps real -world internships, hackathons, and jobs to the student's specific learning progress, ensuring actionable career outcomes. The platform also includes: - **Daily Planner & Spaced Repetition:** AI-managed tasks synchronized with FSRS v5 and Ebisu algorithms. [Page 18] - **Career Pivot Analysis:** Quantifies skill gaps and generates autonomous bridge plans for career transitions. --- ## Powered by Gemma 4 Kalam Spark heavily leverages the **Gemma 4** model family for its reasoning, summarization, and conversational capabilities. To ensure maximum reliability and accessibility, the application features a **3-tier Multi -LLM failover routing system** : 1. **Tier 1 (Primary Cloud):** OpenRouter API (Gemma 4 31B / 26B) 2. **Tier 2 (Secondary Cloud):** Google AI Studio API (Gemma 4 31B) 3. **Tier 3 (Local Fallback):** Local Ollama daemon (`gemma4:e4b`) This architecture guarantees that the platform remains functional even if cloud APIs face rate limits or network issues, allowing users to run the entire AI pipeline entirely locally. --- ## Project Structure & Tech Stack **Frontend:** React 19, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS (Glassmorphism UI) **Backend:** FastAPI (Python 3.11+) **Database & Auth:** Supabase (PostgreSQL) **AI / RAG:** Gemma 4, ChromaDB (Vector Store), `text -embedding -004` [Page 19] **Web Scraping:** Crawl4AI, Playwright, BeautifulSoup **Audio Synthesis:** Edge -TTS (Microsoft Neural voices, supporting 8 Indian languages) --- ## How to Run the Project You can run Kalam Spark in two modes: **Cloud API Mode** (faster, requires API keys) or **Local LLM Mode** (runs completely offline using your hardware). ### Prerequisites - **Node.js** (v18 or higher) - **Python** (v3.11 or higher) - **Supabase Account** (for database setup) ### 1. Database Setup (Supabase) 1. Create a new project on [Supabase]( https://supabase.com/ ). 2. Navigate to the SQL Editor and run the SQL script found in `database/schema.sql` to create the necessary tables. 3. Get your Project URL and Anon Key from the Project Settings -> API. ### 2. Frontend Setup [Page 20] ```bash cd frontend npm install # Setup environment variables cp .env.example .env ``` Edit `frontend/.env` and add your Supabase credentials: ```env VITE_SUPABASE_URL="your -supabase -url" VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="your -supabase -anon -key" VITE_API_URL="http://localhost:8000" ``` Start the frontend development server: ```bash npm run dev # The app will run on http://localhost:5173 (or 3000 depending on Vite config) ``` ### 3. Backend Setup ```bash [Page 21] cd backend # Create and activate a virtual environment python -m venv .venv # Windows: .venv \Scripts \activate # macOS/Linux: source .venv/bin/activate # Install dependencies pip install -r requirements.txt # Install Playwright browser for web crawling playwright install chromium

credentials: ```env VITE_SUPABASE_URL="your -supabase -url" VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="your -supabase -anon -key" VITE_API_URL="http://localhost:8000" ``` Start the frontend development server: ```bash npm run dev # The app will run on http://localhost:5173 (or 3000 depending on Vite config) ``` ### 3. Backend Setup ```bash [Page 21] cd backend # Create and activate a virtual environment python -m venv .venv # Windows: .venv \Scripts \activate # macOS/Linux: source .venv/bin/activate # Install dependencies pip install -r requirements.txt # Install Playwright browser for web crawling playwright install chromium # Setup environment variables cp .env.example .env ``` --- ### Option A: Running with Cloud APIs (Recommended) Edit `backend/.env` and add your API keys. You only need one, but providing both enables failover. [Page 22] ```env OPENROUTER_API_KEY="your -openrouter -key" GEMINI_API_KEY="your -google -ai-studio -key" ``` Start the FastAPI server: ```bash uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload ``` --- ### Option B: Running with Local LLM (Offline Mode via Ollama) Kalam Spark can run entirely offline using Ollama as a fallback when API keys are absent or requests fail. 1. Install [Ollama]( https://ollama.com/ ). 2. Open a new terminal and start the Ollama daemon: ```bash ollama serve ``` 3. Pull the quantized Gemma 4 model: ```bash ollama pull gemma4:e4b [Page 23] ``` 4. Ensure your `backend/.env` has the Ollama configuration (this is usually the default): ```env OLLAMA_BASE_URL="http://localhost:11434" OLLAMA_MODEL="gemma4:e4b" ``` 5. Start the FastAPI server: ```bash uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload ``` _Note: If no cloud API keys are provided in `.env`, the backend will automatically route all AI requests to your local Ollama instance._ --- ## License This project is released under the **Apache License 2.0** . You may obtain a copy of the License at: http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE -2.0 Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied. See the License for the specific language governi ng permissions and limitations under the License. [Page 24] How Act As Agent AI Kalam Spark acts as an Agentic AI platform because it doesn't just "talk" — it performs tasks autonomously by perceiving, reasoning, and acting. Here is the brief breakdown: 1. The Researcher (Career Architect Agent) Instead of just giving you a generic answer, the app acts as a researcher. It uses Crawl4AI to browse the web for real -time industry data, reasons about what is current, and then autonomously designs a custom 4 -stage plan without you telling it what the steps should be. 2. The Stateful Coach (Mentor Agent) Traditional AI forgets you after every chat. This agent maintains a persistent "Student Profile" (your branch, year, and goals). It reasons about your specific context before answering, ensuring its advice is always aligned with your career stage. 3. The Orchestrator (Daily Planner Agent) It takes a high -level goal (e.g., "Become a Data Scientist") and breaks it down into small, executable actions . It then schedules these using spaced -repetition algorithms, managing your "learning lifecycle" autonomously. 4. The Data Transformer (File Speaker Agent) This agent performs complex multimodal workflows. It "reads" your documents (RAG), "understands" the core concepts, and then takes the autonomous action of writing a podcast script and generating audio to teach you that material. In short: It is an agent because it executes multi -step workflows on your behalf to achieve a long -term goal. You can reuse ~70% of your existing code . User Action ↓

concepts, and then takes the autonomous action of writing a podcast script and generating audio to teach you that material. [Page 25] Mobile idea Best Recommended Architecture Option 1: React Native + Cactus (Strongly Recommended) This is the best path because your entire frontend is already React/TypeScript. You can reuse ~70% of your existing code . User Action ↓ React Native App ↓ Try Cloud API (OpenRouter → Google AI Studio) ← internet available ↓ [fails / rate limit / offline] Cactus (llama.cpp on -device) ← runs Gemma 3 -1B or Gemma 2 -2B GGUF Why Cactus? • It's literally llama.cpp compiled for Android (via NDK) and iOS (via Metal) • Has a JavaScript bridge, so it works natively with React Native • Your existing 3 -tier failover logic just adds a 4th tier: on -device Model to use: gemma -3-1b-it.Q4_K_M.gguf (~700MB) — fits comfortably in modern phone RAM Option 2: MediaPipe LLM + LiteRT (Google Native) This is Google's official way to run Gemma on -device . Since your app is already built around Gemma, this is a natural fit. MediaPipe LLM Inference API → Uses LiteRT (TFLite) backend → GPU Delegate (uses phone GPU via OpenGL/Vulkan) [Page 26] → Supports Gemma 2B, Gemma 3 -1B officially Downside: Requires rewriting in Kotlin (Android) or Swift (iOS). Only worth it if building native apps. Role of Unsloth (Bonus Tier) Unsloth is not for mobile inference but you can use it smartly: Fine-tune Gemma -3-1B on career counseling Q&A data using Unsloth → Export to GGUF → Deploy on mobile via Cactus This gives you a smaller, specialized model that outperforms a generic 7B model for your specific use case. Recommended Implementation Roadmap Phase 1 — Port Frontend to React Native ↳ Reuse App.tsx logic, replace DOM -specific APIs Phase 2 — Integrate Cactus for on -device inference ↳ Add Cactus package, download gemma -3-1b.gguf on first launch ↳ Store model in app's local storage (~700MB) Phase 3 — Smart Failover ↳ Cloud APIs (fast, full Gemma 4) ↳ → On -device Cactus (slower, smaller model) Phase 4 (Optional) — Fine-tune with Unsloth ↳ Train career -specific Gemma 1B ↳ Deploy as a domain -expert edge model Other Methods Worth Knowing • MLC -LLM — Compiles LLMs to run via WebGPU in mobile browsers. Zero installation needed. [Page 27] • Executorch (Meta) — PyTorch's mobile export framework, very new but promising. • ONNX Runtime Mobile — Converts models to ONNX format and runs them on -device. Short Answer Use Cactus + React Native. It reuses your existing codebase, runs Gemma on - device as a true offline fallback, and is the fastest path to a working mobile app. Use Unsloth to make a smaller, smarter career -counseling model to deploy through it. The One Best Idea: React Native + Cactus + Gemma 4 (Quantized) The Plan Convert your existing React frontend → React Native app . When offline or API limit hit → run Gemma 4 quantized locally on your phone via Cactus . Yes, You Can Quantize Gemma 4 Gemma 4 full model is too large for mobile. Here's the fix: Gemma 4 Full → ~16GB (too large) Gemma 4 Q4_K_M quantized → ~3 -4GB (fits on modern phones with 8GB RAM) Gemma 4 Q2_K quantized → ~2GB (lower quality but runs on 6GB RAM phones) Use llama.cpp's quantization tool to compress it: bash # Convert and quantize Gemma 4 to GGUF format ./quantize gemma4.gguf gemma4 -Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M [Page

phone via Cactus . Yes, You Can Quantize Gemma 4 Gemma 4 full model is too large for mobile. Here's the fix: Gemma 4 Full → ~16GB (too large) Gemma 4 Q4_K_M quantized → ~3 -4GB (fits on modern phones with 8GB RAM) Gemma 4 Q2_K quantized → ~2GB (lower quality but runs on 6GB RAM phones) Use llama.cpp's quantization tool to compress it: bash # Convert and quantize Gemma 4 to GGUF format ./quantize gemma4.gguf gemma4 -Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M [Page 28] Then Cactus loads this GGUF file on your phone. How to Do This Using Vibe Coding Vibe coding = you describe what you want in plain English to an AI (like me), and it writes all the code for you. Step 1 — Open a new project in Cursor or VS Code with AI Step 2 — Prompt the AI: "Convert my existing React/TypeScript Kalam Spark app to React Native. Keep all the logic the same. Add Cactus integration that loads a GGUF model file and runs it when the API call fails." Step 3 — Prompt for the failover: "Add a 4th fallback tier to my _callLLM function. If the 3 cloud APIs fail, call Cactus on -device inference with the quantized Gemma 4 GGUF model." Step 4 — Prompt for model download: "On first app launch, download gemma4 -Q4_K_M.gguf from Hugging Face and store it in the app's local file system. Show a progress bar during download." The AI writes all the code. You just describe the behavior. Bottom Line Thing Answer Framework React Native On-device engine Cactus (wraps llama.cpp) Model Gemma 4 quantized to Q4_K_M via llama.cpp Model size ~3-4GB on your phone storage Vibe coding tool Cursor / Windsurf + Claude/Gemini [Page 29]
